text: Electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction to fuels and chemicals requires catalysts that not only have high selectivity but also excellent stability and composition using only abundant elements. Here we demonstrate the use of thin films of nanosized metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as catalysts made of atomically and nanoscopically defined composition and demonstrated their use as efficient and selective catalysts for the reduction of CO2 to CO in aqueous solution. A deep analysis of catalytic behavior of a cobalt-based porphyrin-based MOF material, named Al2(OH)2TCPP-Co, where TCPP-H2 corresponds to 4,4',4″,4‴-(porphyrin-5,10,15,20-tetrayl)tetrabenzoate, was performed with a selectivity to CO >76% along with catalytic stability for more than 7 h and with a per-site turnover number (TON) of 1400. Detailed understanding of the catalytic process in this material was attained through in situ spectroelectrochemical studies showing redox-active sites where Co2+ is reduced to Co+ under catalytic conditions and where a significant fraction of the Co species in the MOF framework are under reducing conditions and hence catalytically active.
